text:
Thank you, Brother Lindsay. Good afternoon, or evening, rather. We were kinda all bundled up here tonight, and I was kinda thrilled. I was talking about peppermint candy. How many was at the e--evening service--the afternoon service? I was speaking about peppermint candy. And bless their heart, I found little children standing out there, and give me two big sticks of peppermint candy. That's very fine.
And now, honey, I hope you're listening; I'll just lick it right here tonight. These are all for me. That's all right. That's very fine. I'm blessed of the Lord. I still love peppermint candy anyhow. Very fine.
Can I leave this out, Brother Gordon? If you…?… Oh, yes. That's right. Okay, that's fine. Can you hear all right? All right, just fine.
Now, this is what I always said was a spare tire. Someone asked me, said, "You a preacher, Brother Branham?"
I said, "No, I'm a spare tire." That's when the rest of them's flat. Then we can roll over it. That's it. Thanks.
text_no:
Takk, Bror Lindsay. God ettermiddag, eller kveld, rettere sagt. Vi var alle samlet her i kveld, og jeg var ganske begeistret. Jeg snakket om peppermyntegodteri. Hvor mange var på ettermiddagsmøtet? Jeg snakket om peppermyntegodteri. Og velsigne deres hjerter, jeg fant små barn som sto der og ga meg to store stykker peppermyntegodteri. Det var veldig hyggelig.
Og nå, kjære, jeg håper du lytter; jeg skal bare smake litt på det her i kveld. Disse er alle til meg. Det er helt i orden. Jeg er velsignet av Herren. Jeg elsker fortsatt peppermyntegodteri uansett. Veldig fint.
Kan jeg legge dette til side, Bror Gordon? Hvis du...? Å ja. Det stemmer. Ok, det er greit. Hører du godt? Greit, veldig bra.
Nå, dette er hva jeg alltid har sagt var et reservehjul. Noen spurte meg: "Er du en forkynner, Bror Branham?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei, jeg er et reservehjul." Det er når resten av dem er flate. Da kan vi rulle over det. Det er det. Takk.

text:
Well, we we're supposed to be here a hour…?… And I'm sure that all of you are happy to be here tonight. Drawing near this is the second Sunday night of our tent--service in the tent. And we're going along now a two nights longer, I think, until Thursday night, God willing. And we're expecting God to bless us in something.
Tomorrow night, I have announced that I'm… have something a little different, perhaps, that I could… going to try something different, in a way that I could pray for more sick people each night. Maybe try to get a hundred if possible, each night. That is, if I have--try explain it to you more tomorrow, and I could pray for each individual. Now I believe the faith of the people is high enough now until I can do that, I believe, by the help of the Almighty. God grant it to us.
I wish to read just a little portion of the Word. And just as quickly as possible, you'll have to… Too many…?… Now, in Saint Matthew the 9th chapter, at the 27th verse, we begin to read an instance here, that taken place in the days of our Lord.
text_no:
Vel, vi skulle ha vært her for en time siden... Og jeg er sikker på at dere alle er glade for å være her i kveld. Dette er nå den andre søndagskvelden av våre møter i teltet. Vi fortsetter i to netter til, tror jeg, frem til torsdag kveld, om Gud vil. Vi forventer at Gud vil velsigne oss med noe.
I morgen kveld har jeg annonsert at jeg... har noe litt annerledes, kanskje. Jeg skal prøve noe nytt, en måte hvor jeg kan be for flere syke mennesker hver kveld. Kanskje prøve å få til hundre, om mulig, hver kveld. Jeg skal forklare mer om dette i morgen, slik at jeg kan be for hver enkelt. Nå tror jeg folks tro er sterk nok til at jeg kan gjøre dette, ved den Allmektiges hjelp. Gud gi oss dette.
Jeg ønsker å lese en liten del av Ordet. Og så raskt som mulig, for det er mange... Nå, i Evangeliet etter Matteus, kapittel 9, vers 27, begynner vi å lese en hendelse som fant sted på vår Herres tid.

text:
Now, everyone can hear, all around all right? Can you? Way back in the back row, can you hear? All around. That's very fine.
I'm not a… pulled a lot of…?… anyhow. And I…?… little things in front of you. It's not as strong as the other big night, because…?… Listen closely.
And when Jesus departed thence, two blind men followed him, crying, and saying, Thou son of David, have mercy on us.
And when he was come into the house, the blind men came unto him; and Jesus said unto them, Believe ye that I am able to do this? They said unto him, Yea, Lord. (I like that, don't you?)
Believe ye that I am able to do this? And they said unto him, Yea, Lord.
Then touched he their eyes, saying, According to your faith, be it unto you. (Now, what if they were just did not believe, they would've never been healed.)
And their eyes were opened; and Jesus strictly charged them, saying, See that no man know it. (So they did believe, didn't they?)
But when they were departed, they spread abroad his fame in all of that country.
And as he went out, behold, they brought unto him a dumb man possessed with a devil. (Now, if you'll notice, this…)
[A brother interrupts] All right. That's a whole lot better, isn't it? Thank you. That's very fine. Brings us back home again, doesn't it? That's right. That's just fine.
Now, as I was reading,
And they brought unto him a dumb man possessed with a devil.
And when the devil was cast out, the dumb spake: and the multitude marveled, saying, It was never so seen in Israel.
But the Pharisees said, He casts out devils through the prince of the devils.
And Jesus went about all the cities and villages, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every sickness and disease among the people.
text_no:
Nå, kan alle høre, rundt om, greit? Kan dere det? Helt bakerst, kan dere høre? Rundt om. Det er veldig fint.
Jeg er ikke en… trukket mye av…?… uansett. Og jeg…?… små ting foran deg. Det er ikke like sterkt som den andre store kvelden, fordi…?… Hør nøye etter.
Og da Jesus dro videre derfra, fulgte to blinde menn etter Ham og ropte: Du Davids sønn, miskunn deg over oss.
Og da Han kom inn i huset, kom de blinde til Ham; og Jesus sa til dem: Tror dere at Jeg er i stand til å gjøre dette? De sa til Ham: Ja, Herre. (Jeg liker det, gjør ikke du?)
Tror dere at Jeg er i stand til å gjøre dette? De sa til Ham: Ja, Herre.
Da rørte Han ved øynene deres og sa: Det skal skje dere etter deres tro. (Nå, hva om de bare ikke trodde, de ville aldri blitt helbredet.)
Og deres øyne ble åpnet; og Jesus påla dem strengt og sa: Se til at ingen får vite det. (Så de trodde, ikke sant?)
Men da de dro bort, spredte de Hans berømmelse i hele landet.
Og da Han gikk videre, se, de førte til Ham en stum mann besatt med en djevel. (Nå, hvis dere legger merke til dette…)
[En Bror avbryter] Greit. Det er mye bedre, ikke sant? Takk. Det er veldig fint. Bring oss tilbake hjem igjen, gjør det ikke? Det stemmer. Det er helt fint.
Nå, som jeg leste,
Og de førte til Ham en stum mann besatt med en djevel.
Og da djevelen var kastet ut, talte den stumme; og mengden undret seg og sa: Slik har det aldri vært sett i Israel.
Men fariseerne sa: Han kaster ut djevler ved djevelenes fyrste.
Og Jesus gikk omkring i alle byer og landsbyer, og lærte i deres synagoger, forkynte evangeliet om riket og helbredet enhver sykdom og plage blant folket.

text:
Now, I want you to notice the arrangements of the Scripture here. First, the blind man. And he just…?… Yea, he said, "Lord, have mercy."
And Jesus said, "I can if you believe that I can." (Did you get it?) Then He…
They said, "Lord, we believe."
So He touched their eyes and said, "Now, according to your faith, be it unto you." And they could see.
Then they brought to Him a man that was deaf and dumb. Now, today, it would been… Why, this man, they--his nerves died in his ears. But Jesus called that deaf and dumb person, a spirit, a devil that had deaf and dumb. Now, that's what it was. It was a spirit. The deaf and dumb spirit went out of the man. He could speak and hear when the spirit went out of him. You see what I mean?
But if you say that today, modern meri--meri--medical science would laugh at you, and say, "Oh, spirits. There's no such thing as that."
But we Christians believe the Bible. The Bible said, "It was a spirit." Now, here's what takes place.
text_no:
Vennligst legg merke til oppsettet av Skriften her. Først den blinde mannen. Han sa: "Herre, forbarm Deg."
Jesus svarte: "Jeg kan, hvis du tror at Jeg kan." (Forstår du det?) Deretter...
De sa: "Herre, vi tror."
Så rørte Han ved deres øyne og sa: "Nå, vær det etter deres tro." Og de kunne se.
Så brakte de til Ham en mann som var døv og stum. I dag ville det blitt sagt at mannens nerver i ørene var døde. Men Jesus kalte denne døve og stumme personen for en ånd, en djevel som hadde døv- og stumhet. Det var en ånd. Den døve og stumme ånden forlot mannen. Han kunne tale og høre da ånden forlot ham. Forstår du hva jeg mener?
Men hvis du sier det i dag, ville moderne medisinsk vitenskap le av deg og si: "Ånder? Det finnes ikke noe slikt."
Men vi kristne tror på Bibelen. Bibelen sa at det var en ånd. Nå, her er hva som skjedde.

text:
There's a group, I noticed here somewhere this afternoon… Without the anointing on yet, I--I can't find them. But there's a de--a group of mutes here. Now, I trust to God that they're in the line tonight.
At Portland, Oregon, I had a whole school to come, a whole unit. There was a little scout class, nine or ten of them, that a woman brought them, and each one, one right after the other one. They had prayer cards. They come to the platform, and all nine of them speaking and hearing when they left the platform and returned to their school. And the medical science pronounced them healed. Nine of them. The state's senator was setting up in the balcony, and seen this did.
Now, if you can get the people to see that it's a deaf and dumb spirit… It's a spirit.
text_no:
I ettermiddag la jeg merke til en gruppe her… Uten salvelsen kan jeg ikke finne dem. Men det er en gruppe stumme her. Jeg stoler på Gud at de blir med i køen i kveld.
I Portland, Oregon, kom det en hel skole, en hel enhet. En kvinne tok med seg en liten speiderklasse, ni eller ti elever, hver og en etter tur. De hadde bønnkorkort og kom til plattformen. Da de forlot plattformen, kunne alle ni høre og tale, og de returnerte til skolen. Medisinsk vitenskap erklærte dem helbredet – alle ni. Delstatens senator satt oppe i balkongen og så dette skje.
Hvis du kan få folk til å forstå at det er en døv og stum ånd… Det er en ånd.

text:
Now, looky here. Like I explained the other night. If something was cutting off the circulation in my hand… Now, there's only one way to ever get that out, and that is to release this. And then nature will take care of itself as long as that binding comes off.
Well, that's the same way it is when an unseen force binds the ears, or the tongue, or some limb of a person. Now, you take people that's bound. Now, it's hard to tell that person. Maybe a Christian, renown, full of the Spirit of God, but yet, bound. Now, the…
text_no:
Se her. Som jeg forklarte den andre kvelden: Hvis noe hindret blodomløpet i hånden min, er det bare én måte å få det bort på, og det er ved å løsne dette. Da vil naturen ta vare på seg selv så lenge hindringen fjernes.
Det er det samme når en usynlig kraft binder noen sine ører, tungen eller en annen kroppsdel. Ta for eksempel mennesker som er bundet. Det kan være vanskelig å forklare det til dem, kanskje de er kristne, anerkjente og fylt av Guds Ånd, men likevel bundet. Nå, den...

text:
Someone made a remark in a paper not long ago: said, "Well, the people are all going down to the Branham healing campaign and getting de-demonized." Said, "He takes… He--he detects all the demons and casts them all out."
Now, that was a very flat thing for someone to say. But through ignorance, the man done it. If he'd have only just set down and read the Bible a minute, he would find out. Now, he did that… just said that because he didn't know any better.
And if he did know better, God help his soul, 'cause he's blasphemed the Holy Ghost then. And there's never will be a forgiveness for that. See, "Whosoever blasphemes the Holy Ghost…" It's a unpardonable sin. It can never be forgiven at all. Through all eternity, you're still lost forever. To speak it--you don't just speak against the Holy Ghost. See? So be careful. If you don't understand it, just don't say nothing, just go ahead. See? Let it alone, 'cause…
Oh, my, the things that I have seen happen, friends, in that.
text_no:
For en tid tilbake gjorde noen en bemerkning i en avis: "Alle drar til Branham-helbredelseskampanjen og blir kvitt demonene." Han sa: "Han oppdager alle demonene og kaster dem ut." 
Denne uttalelsen var ganske upresis. Mannen sa det i uvitenhet. Hadde han bare satt seg ned og lest Bibelen litt, ville han ha forstått. Han sa det simpelthen fordi han ikke visste bedre. 
Hvis han faktisk visste bedre, må Gud hjelpe hans sjel, for da har han spottet Den Hellige Ånd. Og for det finnes det ingen tilgivelse. "Den som spotter Den Hellige Ånd..." er det utilgivelige synd. Det kan aldri bli tilgitt. Gjennom all evighet er du fortapt. Å uttale seg mot Den Hellige Ånd... Det må man være svært forsiktig med. Hvis du ikke forstår det, si ingenting og la det være i fred.
Åh, min, de tingene jeg har sett skje, venner, i denne sammenhengen.

text:
But the Bible plainly states that they are demons. And they bind people. Look at the woman, the daughter of Israel. She was all bound over, bent over. And Jesus said, "Which one of you, having a…" [Blank.spot.on.tape] Said, "How much more is this daughter of Israel, who's been bound by the devil?" Stooped over, perhaps arthritis.
Today, the medical doctors would say she had arthritis. That's the medical name. But Jesus would've declared it to be a devil.
Today, cancer, cancer is a medical name, comes from the word "crab," got lot of legs. They call it cancer. That's medical name. Jesus would've said, "devil."
text_no:
Bibelen sier klart at de er demoner og at de binder mennesker. Se på kvinnen, datteren av Israel. Hun var sammenbøyd og bundet. Jesus sa: "Hvem av dere, som har en…" [Tomt.område.på.lydbånd] Sa: "Hvor mye mer denne datteren av Israel, som har vært bundet av djevelen?" Hun var kanskje krumbøyd på grunn av leddgikt.
I dag vil legene si at hun hadde leddgikt. Det er den medisinske betegnelsen. Men Jesus ville ha erklært det som en demon. I dag, kreft, kreft er en medisinsk betegnelse, kommer fra ordet "krabbe," som har mange ben. De kaller det kreft. Det er den medisinske betegnelsen. Jesus ville ha sagt: "demon."

text:
Of course, if anyone with a common school education knows a "devil" means "tormentor." You've got a tormentor in your soul, see your pastor. You've got a torment in your body, that's what I'm here for. See? All right. Tormentor, devil…
Epilepsy, that's a name the medical science gives today. When the man brought an epileptic to Jesus, he said, "My son has a devil. And oft times he throws him into the fire and into the water." See what it was? Falling, pines away, faints out: epilepsy. He said, "The devil throws him in."
And Jesus cast the spirit out, said, "Come out of him." Is that right?
The boy had the hardest fit, I guess, he ever had. He fell on the floor and was frothing there on the ground. They thought he was dead. But He said, "He's not dead." And He took him up. He was just… The devil had throwed him into that tantrum before he left him, tore him. Many times it happens.
text_no:
Selvfølgelig, alle med grunnleggende skolegang vet at "djevel" betyr "plageånd." Hvis du har en plageånd i sjelen din, gå til din pastor. Hvis du har en plage i kroppen, er det derfor jeg er her. Forstår du? Greit. Plageånd, djevel...
Epilepsi er navnet medisinsk vitenskap bruker i dag. Da mannen brakte en epileptisk til Jesus, sa han, "Min sønn har en djevel. Og ofte kaster den ham i ilden og i vannet." Ser du hva det var? Falt, svimte av: epilepsi. Han sa, "Djevelen kaster ham i."
Og Jesus kastet ånden ut, og sa, "Kom ut av ham." Stemmer det?
Gutten hadde sannsynligvis sitt verste anfall noen gang. Han falt på gulvet og fikk fråde rundt munnen. De trodde han var død. Men Jesus sa, "Han er ikke død." Og Han løftet ham opp. Djevelen hadde kastet ham inn i det anfallet før han forlot ham, rev og slet i ham. Dette skjer ofte.

text:
Today, if a Christian now, when they come and the demon's been cast from them, and they take worse right away, they say, "Oh, I failed to get healed."
You believe what's told you. Faith comes by hearing, hearing of the Word. And God's here with His gift confirming the Word with signs following. See what I mean?
One of the most marvelous things that I could think of right now, for just a little instance, just before coming. I was… Well, I always… I'll be get something else, so that it'll… It won't take me just a moment.
Back in the first part of my ministry… I don't know what these ministers here tell of other things. But this is one thing that happened, that I know none of them here was with me when this happened.
text_no:
I dag, hvis en kristen har blitt befridd fra en demon og umiddelbart blir verre, sier de ofte: "Å, jeg klarte ikke å bli helbredet."
Du må tro på det som blir fortalt deg. Tro kommer av å høre, og å høre Guds Ord. Gud er her med Sin gave og bekrefter Ordet med tegn som følger. Skjønner du hva jeg mener?
En av de mest fantastiske tingene jeg kan tenke på akkurat nå, er en liten hendelse som skjedde rett før jeg kom hit. Jeg var... Vel, jeg kommer alltid til å dele noe annet, slik at det ikke vil ta meg lang tid.
Tilbake i begynnelsen av min tjeneste... Jeg vet ikke hva disse forkynnerne her sier om andre ting, men dette er en hendelse som skjedde, og jeg vet at ingen av dem her var med meg da det skjedde.

text:
It was about the third meeting I ever had. And my wife came down. She--she's the most backward bash… She's worse than my mother. And you know she's bashful. And I told her; I said, "I'm going to introduce you to the people tonight." And she was afraid to come, afraid I'd really do it. She's just bashful and backward, timid little country girl. And she--she came to the meeting. And she came down to meet me.
And we'd been having there at the tabernacle, oh, three or four hundred people, or a thousand, maybe, on a big night. But this time there was thousands there.
The other night, at the same meeting I was telling you about, the newspaper they carried where that woman had raised up from the dead, Mrs. Hattie Waldorf. The cancer dropped out of a man's neck, rolled across my foot? You remember me telling that just a few nights ago? "Arkansas Sun," from Jonesboro carried the article. Was in this meeting. And Jones… Great big cross there… Twenty-six thousand people at the meeting attended.
text_no:
Det var omtrent det tredje møtet jeg noen gang hadde. Og min kone kom ned. Hun er den mest tilbakeholdne personen jeg kjenner; hun er verre enn min mor. Og du vet at hun er sjenert. Jeg sa til henne: "Jeg skal introdusere deg for folkene i kveld." Hun var redd for å komme, redd for at jeg virkelig skulle gjøre det. Hun er bare en sjenert og tilbakeholden, forsiktig liten bygdejente. Men hun kom til møtet for å møte meg.
Vi hadde hatt rundt tre eller fire hundre, eller kanskje tusen på en stor kveld, på tabernaklet. Men denne gangen var det tusenvis der.
For noen kvelder siden, på det samme møtet jeg fortalte dere om, skrev avisen om at en kvinne hadde blitt oppreist fra de døde, Fru Hattie Waldorf. Kreften falt ut av en manns hals og rullet over foten min. Husker du at jeg fortalte om det for bare noen kvelder siden? "Arkansas Sun" fra Jonesboro skrev om det. Jones... En stor kors der... Tjueseks tusen mennesker deltok på møtet.

text:
And so the newspaper reporter was standing as close as that pole when that cancer fell out of the man's neck and rolled across my foot. And I picked it up and laid it on a piece of paper. And he was afraid to put his hands on it. And he asked the man if he could get the picture.
He said, "Yes." But he took the picture of his neck where it dropped out: a minister.
I seen a man one night with one on the side of his nose, looked like a great seed wart, white about like that. It's… On the ends of it. The inside, it was real bloody and red, just like this was. And while I was praying for him, the cancer left. And no one seen where it went. His face was as smooth…
text_no:
Avisreporteren sto like ved stolpen da kreften falt ut av mannens nakke og rullet bortover foten min. Jeg plukket den opp og la den på et papirark. Reporteren var redd for å ta på den og ba mannen om å få ta et bilde.
Mannen svarte: "Ja," men reporteren tok bilde av nakken der kreften hadde falt ut. Mannen var en predikant.
En kveld så jeg en mann med noe på siden av nesen, som så ut som en stor vortelignende utvekst, hvit i endene og blodig og rød på innsiden, akkurat som denne. Mens jeg ba for ham, forsvant kreften, og ingen så hvor den ble av. Ansiktet hans ble glatt...

text:
In the same meeting, I'd seen a woman come in with her handkerchief over her face. That's the meeting I stayed eight days and nights without leaving. And a woman come; she had her handkerchief over her face, like this. I thought she was weeping. It's was along three or four o'clock in the morning. I took ahold of her hand. And I thought she was weeping.
I said, "Lady, why the vibration is cancer." She moved her handkerchief down; her nose was eaten off, young woman, about thirty-eight years old, forty. So I said, "Oh, my."
And she said, "I've just been give up by the best of doctors. It's done too far gone now. They can't do nothing. They've fought it with the X-ray treatments, radium, and everything. But it's too far gone."
I said, "Do you believe?"
She said, "With all my heart."
I said, "Will you take my word for what I say?"
She said, "I will."
I said, "Let us pray." And I prayed. The vibrations stopped. I said, "Sister, there's only one thing that I can tell you; you just have my word. The blessing of God is upon you. If you'll accept it and accept my word, and believe that I've told you what God has said in my heart, you'll get well."
text_no:
På det samme møtet så jeg en kvinne komme inn med et lommetørkle over ansiktet. Det var møtet hvor jeg ble i åtte dager og netter uten å forlate. Kvinnen kom inn med et lommetørkle over ansiktet. Jeg trodde hun gråt. Det var rundt tre-fire om natten. Jeg tok hånden hennes og trodde hun gråt.
Jeg sa, "Dame, vibrasjonene indikerer kreft." Hun fjernet lommetørkleet; nesen hennes var spist bort, hun var en ung kvinne, omtrent tretti-åtte år, førti. Jeg sa, "Å, min."
Hun sa, "De beste legene har gitt meg opp. Det er for langt gått nå. De kan ikke gjøre noe. De har kjempet mot det med røntgenbehandling, radium og alt mulig. Men det er for sent."
Jeg spurte, "Tror du?"
Hun svarte, "Av hele mitt hjerte."
Jeg sa, "Vil du stole på mitt ord?"
Hun sa, "Ja, det vil jeg."
Jeg sa, "La oss be." Og jeg ba. Vibrasjonene stoppet. Jeg sa, "Søster, det eneste jeg kan fortelle deg er at du må ta mitt ord. Guds velsignelse er over deg. Hvis du aksepterer det og stoler på mitt ord, og tror at jeg har fortalt deg hva Gud har lagt på hjertet mitt, vil du bli frisk."

text:
And about two months after that, I was in a certain place, and here was a young fellow standing there. He said--raised right up in the meeting. He said, "Can I testify, Reverend Branham?"
And I said, "What is it? Go ahead." I said, "Say on."
And he said, "Looky here. Do you remember…" Said, "I'm an exterminator from--from Texarkana." Said, "My mother setting here, about two months ago come to you with no nose." Said, "I want you to look at her now," just as perfect as she could be.
Well, that's right. And he said, "I've got the doctor's statement right here too with it." And… where it was. And her picture before and after, where it was, a statement.
text_no:
Rundt to måneder senere var jeg på et bestemt sted, og der sto en ung mann. Han reiste seg opp midt i møtet og sa: "Kan jeg vitne, Broder Branham?"
Jeg svarte: "Hva gjelder det? Gå i vei." Jeg sa: "Fortsett."
Han sa: "Se her. Husker du meg? Jeg er en skadedyrsbekjemper fra Texarkana. Min mor, som sitter her i dag, kom til deg for rundt to måneder siden uten nese. Jeg vil at du skal se på henne nå," sa han. Hennes nese var nå helt normal.
Han tilføyde: "Jeg har legens erklæring her også," og viste hvor det sto. Han hadde også bilder av henne før og etter, sammen med en erklæring om tilstanden hennes.

text:
In the meeting, wife came down, and the baby was just a little bitty thing then, just about three, four months old. And we… She came down that night. We went… We'd went out to the train to pick her up. I went to the service that night. And before we got there, about two city blocks away, it was just like it's in here--the people.
Those Arkansas people… Is there… They--there's some of here, I'm sure. I've never had a meeting, even in Canada but what there's somebody from Arkansas. Anybody here originally from Arkansas? Let's see your hands. That's right. I know that. That's right. I know. See? I've never seen…
text_no:
På møtet kom min kone ned, og babyen var bare en liten skapning da, omtrent tre til fire måneder gammel. Den kvelden kom hun ned. Vi dro ut til toget for å hente henne. Jeg gikk til møtet den kvelden. Og før vi kom dit, omtrent to kvartaler unna, var det like mange folk som her inne.
Disse menneskene fra Arkansas… Er det noen her fra Arkansas? Jeg er sikker på det er noen. Jeg har aldri hatt et møte, selv i Canada, uten at det var noen fra Arkansas. Er det noen her som opprinnelig er fra Arkansas? Rekk opp hånden. Der ser man. Jeg visste det. Jeg har aldri sett...

text:
I was going to ask them in London, England, and Sweden; anywhere you go, you find people from Arkansas. Where I was over on the west coast, and I said there one night where I was having services, where Charles Fuller at the Long Beach Municipal Auditorium… Brother Fuller said, "Brother Branham, the people over here will never come to hear such fanaticism."
I said, "Oh, they won't?"
"No." Said, "You'd better preach the Gospel while you're over here."
I said, "That's what I'm doing."
He said, "Well," he said, "that's Divine healing."
I said, "Brother Fuller, I belong to the Baptist church too. I know how you're feel about it." But I said, "Let me tell you something."
He said, "Brother Branham, everything over here is Divine healing." Said, "People won't come out." He said, "First is that McPherson movement. Next was that fanatic Pentecost." He said, "And all up-and-down this street here and everything's got Divine healing." And he said, "People won't come out. You're renting that auditorium for nearly a thousand dollars." He said, "You're going to be left alone."
I said, "Look, Brother Fuller," I said, "God will recognize His gift if it was in the middle of hell." See? I said, "Don't worry about that."
He said, "Well, I can't advertise it."
I said, "I never asked you to, Brother Fuller." I just talking to you. Said, "Come on down and kick your hat in." I said, "I'll be there."
But he said, "I'm telling you before, you'll be left without."
We went down there that afternoon, Brother Moore, and a group of us. Had about eighteen, twenty-hundred people setting around. And when his group went out, he had his sermon…
text_no:
Jeg skulle spørre dem i London, England og Sverige; uansett hvor du drar, finner du folk fra Arkansas. En gang var jeg på vestkysten, og en kveld sa jeg mens jeg holdt møter, på samme sted som Charles Fuller i Long Beach Municipal Auditorium... Bror Fuller sa, "Bror Branham, folk her kommer aldri til å høre på slik fanatisme."
Jeg sa, "Å, gjør de ikke det?"
"Nei." Sa han, "Du bør heller forkynne Evangeliet mens du er her."
Jeg sa, "Det er det jeg gjør."
Han sa, "Vel," han sa, "det der er guddommelig helbredelse."
Jeg sa, "Bror Fuller, jeg tilhører også baptistkirken. Jeg vet hvordan du føler om det." Men jeg sa, "La meg fortelle deg noe."
Han sa, "Bror Branham, alt her handler om guddommelig helbredelse." Sa, "Folk vil ikke komme." Han sa, "Først var det McPherson-bevegelsen. Deretter var det den fanatiske pinsebevegelsen." Han sa, "Og overalt langs denne gaten handler alt om guddommelig helbredelse." Og han sa, "Folk kommer ikke. Du leier det auditoriet for nesten tusen dollar." Han sa, "Du blir stående alene."
Jeg sa, "Se, Bror Fuller," jeg sa, "Gud vil anerkjenne Sin gave selv om det var midt i helvete." Ser du? Jeg sa, "Ikke bekymre deg for det."
Han sa, "Vel, jeg kan ikke reklamere for det."
Jeg sa, "Jeg ba deg aldri om det, Bror Fuller." Jeg bare snakket med deg. Sa, "Kom ned og bidra." Jeg sa, "Jeg vil være der."
Men han sa, "Jeg sier deg på forhånd, du vil bli stående alene."
Vi dro ned dit den ettermiddagen, Bror Moore, og en gruppe av oss. Det var rundt 1800-2000 mennesker til stede. Og da hans gruppe forlot, hadde han sin preken...

text:
Now, remember, Charles Fuller is a wonderful, God-sent man, a real Christian man. Don't know whether you ever got his program or not. He has the "Old Fashion Revival Hour," which is a very fine program. Wonderful, marvelous teacher, a good man, aged, he's getting pretty aged. But he's a good man. I believe he's a real Christian.
And when we was leaving, Brother Fuller was going out, Brother Moore and I were standing there. And there went his audience out after he made a fine talk. And a lady came down, shook hands with him to give her heart to Christ, which is wonderful.
He was going to start out. And there when all of his crowd out, fine dressed people, you know, very highly educated. He was standing there telling them, "Just you believe right now, and be saved. That's all." See? Then when his crowd went--going out, here come my crowd in--wheelchairs, straight jackets.
And brother, it's a different thing when your faith has to buck something like that. Just preach, speaking to a crowd, and tell them to raise up their hand and accept Christ. He turned pale looking.
That night, there was so many people there, they stood plumb down on the side of the beach. A fifty-five hundred auditorium wouldn't even seat half of them. They was standing out everywhere.
I tell you, where the carcass is, the eagles will be gathered. You can depend…
text_no:
Husk at Charles Fuller er en fantastisk, Gud-send mann, en ekte kristen. Jeg vet ikke om du har fått med deg programmet hans, "Old Fashion Revival Hour," men det er et veldig fint program. Enestående, vidunderlig lærer og et godt menneske. Han begynner å bli ganske gammel, men han er fortsatt en god mann. Jeg tror han er en ekte kristen.
Da vi skulle dra, var Bror Fuller på vei ut, og Bror Moore og jeg sto der. Fullers publikum hadde akkurat gått etter at han hadde holdt en fin tale. En dame kom ned, tok ham i hånden og gav sitt hjerte til Kristus, noe som er vidunderlig.
Når hans publikum, som var pent kledd og svært godt utdannet, var på vei ut, sto han der og sa til dem: "Bare tro nå, og bli frelst. Det er alt." Da hans folk gikk ut, kom mitt folk inn - folk i rullestoler og tvangstrøyer.
Min bror, det er en annen sak når troen din må stå opp mot en slik situasjon. Bare det å forkynne for en folkemengde og be dem løfte hånden og akseptere Kristus. Fuller ble blek da han så det.
Den kvelden var det så mange mennesker der at de sto helt ned på stranden. En sal med plass til fem tusen fem hundre kunne ikke engang romme halvparten av dem. De sto overalt.
Jeg sier deg, der hvor åtslet er, vil ørnene samles. Du kan stole på det...

text:
Notice, wife was there that night when we looked and seen all that mass of people standing there, Jonesboro. She said, "Honey, did all those come to hear you preach?"
I said, "No. They've come from the east and west. They come from the north and south, and the lands afar to feast with the King, to dine as His guests." I said, "They're children of the King." I said, "They're not here to see me, honey. They're here to see Jesus."
She said, "Well, how will you ever get to the auditorium?"
I said, "There'll be men to meet me at the bottom of the hill here."
I looked at that crowd; I said, raised up my hands. I said, "Dear dying Lamb, Thy precious Blood shall never lose its power till all the ransomed church of God be saved to sin no more."
The Gospel is the greatest drawing card the world's ever had. That's right. Though preached in its simplicity… "If I be lifted up, I'll draw all men unto Me." That's true.
text_no:
Min kone var til stede den kvelden da vi så alle de menneskene, i Jonesboro. Hun sa: "Kjære, har alle disse kommet for å høre deg preke?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei. De har kommet fra øst og vest, fra nord og sør, og fra fjerne land for å feste med Kongen, for å spise som Hans gjester." Jeg sa: "De er barn av Kongen. De er ikke her for å se meg, kjære. De er her for å se Jesus."
Hun spurte: "Hvordan skal du komme deg til auditoriet?"
Jeg svarte: "Det vil være menn som møter meg ved bunnen av bakken her."
Jeg så på mengden og løftet hendene. Jeg sa: "Kjære, døende Lam, Dit dyrebare blod skal aldri miste sin kraft før hele den forløste menigheten av Gud er frelst til å synde ikke mer."
Evangeliet er det største trekkplasteret verden noensinne har hatt. Det er sant. Selv når det forkynnes i sin enkelhet ... "Hvis Jeg blir løftet opp, vil Jeg trekke alle mennesker til Meg." Det er sant.

text:
A lady that afternoon, had been blind. She walked out, and she seen the trees and things. She went over there at the graveyard, looked at the tombstones where some of her people had been buried. Years since she'd seen…
So four men… I never seen what become of the wife after that. They was pressing me in towards the crowd. There's probably two thousand people standing against the door, like that. And so we finally squeezed through the crowd until we got on the inside. And I hadn't slept. I was just so weary; I lost about twenty-eight pounds of weight. And I was pressing into the crowd, them great big strong fellows pushing me through.
And I got up on the platform, there was eighteen or twenty stretcher cases setting right along like that. And setting right here was a little girl. She was tubercular case. Her nurses was with her.
text_no:
En dame hadde vært blind den ettermiddagen. Hun gikk ut og så trærne og omgivelsene. Hun gikk til gravlunden og så på gravstøttene der noen av hennes slektninger var begravet. År hadde gått siden hun sist hadde sett...
Fire menn presset meg inn mot mengden. Jeg vet ikke hva som skjedde med kona etter det. Det var sannsynligvis omtrent to tusen mennesker som sto mot døren. Vi presset oss gjennom mengden og kom endelig inn. Jeg hadde ikke sovet og var svært utmattet; jeg hadde mistet omtrent tjueåtte pund. De sterke mennene presset meg gjennom folkemengden.
Da jeg kom opp på plattformen, så jeg atten eller tjue pasienter på bårer ligge langs den ene siden. Og der satt en liten jente, en tuberkulosepasient, med sykepleierne sine ved sin side.

text:
And I just got to the platform, and I started to look down, and I could just feel the vibration coming.
There'd been a great thing happen there that afternoon. There was a lady come across the platform, another instance. I took ahold of her hand. She had a female vibration. I said, "You have a female trouble."
She had a little boy on her arm. She said, "Yes, sir."
I said, "You believe me?"
She said, "With all my heart."
I took ahold of her hand, and I begin to pray. It wouldn't leave her. And I said, "Sister, I don't understand why. Are you a Christian?"
She said, "Yes, sir."
text_no:
Så snart jeg kom opp på plattformen, begynte jeg å se ned, og jeg kunne føle vibrasjonene komme.
Tidligere på ettermiddagen hadde det skjedd noe stort der. En dame kom over plattformen i en annen sammenheng. Jeg tok henne i hånden og kjente en kvinnelig vibrasjon. Jeg sa: "Du har en kvinnesykdom."
Hun hadde en liten gutt på armen. Hun svarte: "Ja, sir."
Jeg spurte: "Tror du meg?"
Hun svarte: "Av hele mitt hjerte."
Jeg tok igjen hånden hennes og begynte å be. Vibrasjonen ville ikke forsvinne. Jeg sa: "Søster, jeg forstår ikke hvorfor. Er du en kristen?"
Hun svarte: "Ja, sir."

text:
And I just looked at her. There it moved out. A vision begin to move.
Now, remember, that woman standing there was told of living untrue to her marriage vows four days before that, before the audience, twenty thousand people or better, listening.
And she started crying. The man behind her was her husband. He jerked her around, said, "What's this?" (You see why I have to pray often?) "What's this?"
She said, "Darling, I'm sorry."
Then It just… I just let myself loose. It moved over on him, and told him of the same act with his bookkeeper, and the car they were in, and where they were at.
He turned white. He said, "Brother, what must I do?" I looked…
He looked at her, and he reached his hand out, said, "Darling, I've been a rat." Said, "If you forgive me, I'll forgive you. And we'll start life anew right here before God's people."
She said, "I forgive you."
And they put the little boy between them, and hugged one another, knelt down on the floor and give their hearts to God.
And Reverend Richard T. Reed, of Jonesboro, Arkansas, baptized them in Christian faith. The next morning they were added to the church. That's the way the Lord works. Truly.
text_no:
Jeg så bare på henne. Der begynte en visjon å komme til syne.
Husk at denne kvinnen, som sto der, fire dager tidligere foran et publikum på over tyve tusen mennesker, ble avslørt for å ha vært utro mot sine ekteskapsløfter.
Hun begynte å gråte. Mannen bak henne var hennes ektemann. Han rykket henne rundt og sa: "Hva er dette?" (Ser du hvorfor jeg må be ofte?) "Hva er dette?"
Hun sa: "Kjære, jeg er lei meg."
Så lot jeg meg selv slippe løs. Visjonen dreide seg over til ham og fortalte om den samme handlingen med hans bokholder, bilen de var i, og hvor de var.
Han ble hvit i ansiktet. Han sa: "Bror, hva skal jeg gjøre?"
Han så på henne, rakte ut hånden og sa: "Kjære, jeg har vært en rotte. Hvis du tilgir meg, vil jeg tilgi deg. Og vi starter livet på nytt her foran Guds folk."
Hun sa: "Jeg tilgir deg."
De satte den lille gutten mellom seg, omfavnet hverandre, knelte på gulvet og ga sine hjerter til Gud.
Og pastor Richard T. Reed fra Jonesboro, Arkansas, døpte dem i kristen tro. Neste morgen ble de tatt opp i menigheten. Slik virker Herren, virkelig.

text:
Now, when they was at the platform, this little girl was looking. And I looked up at her. And I looked down to her, rather. And she was looking up at me. And I kept seeing someone standing to my right, kept motioning their hands, like this. And I looked down there, and I seen some people rejoicing.
There'd been a blind man from up in Kennett, was healed that night before that. And he was going on his road home, been drawing a blind pension for twelve years. And he was healed. I asked God to bless him. I said, "Do you believe?"
He said, "With all my heart."
I said, "Go on rejoicing, testifying of your healing."
They led him out to an old Ford. He started up the road, about a hundred and something miles up to Kennett, Missouri. And he was going up the road, praising God for his sight, just as blind as he could be. All at once, he screamed. He said, "Stop the car. I see the lights." He jumped out of the car, up-and-down the road he went, jumping and screaming.
He went into Kennett, just… Or at the Catholic church was having mass, put his cane up in the air and his hat on there. He walked down through the Catholic church, screaming, "I'm healed. I'm healed by the glory of God." Out into the Methodist church he went. Up-and-down the streets he…?… the country there. They were testifying everywhere of his healing.
text_no:
Da de stod på plattformen, så denne lille jenta opp. Jeg så ned på henne, og vi møttes blikk. Hele tiden så jeg noen stå til høyre for meg, som viftet med hendene, slik som dette. Jeg så ned og la merke til noen mennesker som jublet.
En blind mann fra Kennett hadde blitt helbredet kvelden før. Han hadde mottatt blindepensjon i tolv år. Gud helbredet ham. Jeg ba Gud velsigne ham og spurte, "Tror du?"
Han svarte, "Med hele mitt hjerte."
Jeg sa, "Fortsett å glede deg og vitne om din helbredelse."
De ledet ham ut til en gammel Ford. Han begynte på veien hjem, omtrent hundre mil opp til Kennett, Missouri. Mens han kjørte, priste han Gud for synet sitt, selv om han fortsatt var like blind. Plutselig skrek han, "Stopp bilen. Jeg ser lysene." Han hoppet ut av bilen og sprang opp og ned veien, hoppende og skrikende.
Da han kom til Kennett, der den katolske menigheten holdt messe, løftet han stokken og hatten i lufta. Han gikk gjennom den katolske menigheten, ropende, "Jeg er helbredet. Jeg er helbredet ved Guds herlighet." Deretter gikk han inn i metodistmenigheten. På gatene og på landsbygda vitnet han overalt om sin helbredelse.

text:
Looking out to one side, I seen a man motioning his hand, like this. And I thought it looked like a ambulance driver. He had on a blue looking uniform. And I said, "Are you motioning for me, sir?"
He said, "Yes, sir." Said, "Brother Branham," said, "I've brought my patient down from Kennett, where the blind man was healed this morning." He said, "And I've got to make another trip tonight from the hospital." And said, "I think my patient's dead." (We had to have a house doctor there. There's so many sick and things; you have to have a house doctor. We couldn't find him anywhere.) Said, "I think my patient's dead. Won't you come to her?"
And I said, "Brother, I'd like to go. But how could I get through that crowd? There's probably two or three thousand people between here and there."
text_no:
Mens jeg kikket til siden, så jeg en mann vinke med hånden på denne måten. Jeg trodde han så ut som en ambulansesjåfør. Han hadde på seg en blå uniform. Jeg spurte: "Vinker du til meg, sir?"
Han svarte: "Ja, sir. Bror Branham, jeg har tatt med meg min pasient fra Kennett, der den blinde mannen ble helbredet i morges. Nå må jeg gjøre en ny tur i kveld fra sykehuset, og jeg tror pasienten min er død." (Vi måtte ha en huslege til stede på grunn av de mange syke; det er nødvendig med en huslege. Vi kunne ikke finne ham noe sted.) Han sa: "Jeg tror pasienten min er død. Vil du komme til henne?"
Jeg svarte: "Bror, jeg skulle gjerne gått, men hvordan kan jeg komme meg gjennom folkemengden? Det er sannsynligvis to til tre tusen mennesker mellom her og der."

text:
They had a row reserved for ambulances out there. Many of them, eighteen or twenty of them setting in that row. And I said, "Well, I--I couldn't get out there."
Four men stepped down out of the balcony, said, "We'll take you, Brother Branham, if you wish to go."
I said, "Well, if you'll sing a hymn while I'm gone, or something."
One of the ministers stepped up to the microphone. And they started me through the crowd. Oh, my. I was pushing, and people crying and begging. Oh, you know. We got out to the ambulance; they opened the door. And he stepped to the door. And one sight that I shall never forget…
text_no:
Det var en rad reservert for ambulanser der ute, med atten eller tjue ambulanser på rekke. Jeg sa: "Vel, jeg—jeg kunne ikke komme meg dit ut."
Fire menn kom ned fra balkongen og sa: "Vi tar deg med, Bror Branham, hvis du ønsker å gå."
Jeg svarte: "Vel, hvis dere kan synge en salme mens jeg er borte, eller noe lignende."
En av predikantene gikk frem til mikrofonen, og de begynte å bane vei for meg gjennom folkemengden. Å, min Gud. Folk presset på, gråt og ba. Vi kom oss til ambulansen; de åpnet døren for meg. Han gikk til inngangen, og det synet skal jeg aldri glemme...

text:
Sometime when I can get alone up in the mountains, I set down and think these things over. Now, I don't have time, hardly. And there was a dear old Arkansas brother, very typical, an old faded out blue shirt on, been washed many times, his hat, sewed, the top of it with twine, wrapping cord, his shoes, the soles of them about off. And he was kneeling down on the floor, gripping this hat, and needed a shave real bad, and the tears rolling down his face, saying, "God, give me mother back."
And I looked, laying there, there laid a woman with her mouth open, her eyes open, also set back.
And the intern said, "Here's Brother Branham."
text_no:
Noen ganger, når jeg kan være alene oppe i fjellene, setter jeg meg ned og tenker over disse tingene. Nå har jeg knapt tid. Det var en kjær gammel bror fra Arkansas, veldig typisk, med en gammel, utvasket blå skjorte, mange ganger vasket, en hatt som var sydd i toppen med hyssing, og sko hvor sålene nesten var borte. Han knelte på gulvet, grep hatten sin, trengte sårt en barbering, og tårene rant nedover ansiktet hans mens han sa: "Gud, gi meg mor tilbake."
Jeg så en kvinne ligge der med åpen munn og øyne som var stivnet.
En turnuskandidat sa: "Her er Bror Branham."

text:
He turned around, said, "Oh, Brother Branham," said, "how I want--wanted… She wanted to see you." Said, "She's a good woman, Brother Branham." Said, "She's helped… She's raised five children." Said, "They're all grown and married." Said, "Now we…" Said, "We've got her a little place." And said, "She… We picked blackberries last summer," and said, "together." And said, "She made some quilts." And said, "She's been so bad with cancer," said, "they've give her up. And we sold the blackberries yesterday, and some of her quilts so we could have the money to pay the ambulance to bring us down here." Said, "She wanted to see you so bad. And she's gone, Brother Branham. It was her last request. I've done all I could do, sir."
And I said, "God bless you, dad." I put my hand… I said, "You'll see her in a better land then."
He said, "Yes, she was a Christian, Brother Branham. But oh, she wanted to see you so bad."
text_no:
Han snudde seg og sa: "Å, Bror Branham, hun ønsket virkelig å se deg." Han fortsatte: "Hun er en god kvinne, Bror Branham. Hun har oppdratt fem barn, og de er alle voksne og gift. Nå har vi skaffet henne et lite sted. Vi plukket bjørnebær sammen forrige sommer, og hun laget noen dyner. Hun har hatt det svært vondt med kreft, og legene har gitt opp håpet. Vi solgte bjørnebærene og noen av dynene i går for å ha penger til å betale ambulansen for å bringe oss hit. Hun ønsket så sterkt å se deg. Nå er hun borte, Bror Branham. Det var hennes siste ønske. Jeg har gjort alt jeg kunne, sir."
Jeg svarte: "Gud velsigne deg, far." Jeg la hånden på ham og sa: "Du vil se henne i et bedre land."
Han sa: "Ja, hun var en kristen, Bror Branham. Men å, hun ønsket virkelig å se deg."

text:
I said, "All right." I said… Took a hold of her hand. Now, I do not know whether the woman was gone or not. God is my… I think she was in a coma. I'm not sure. Although I couldn't feel her heart, nor nothing.
But now listen. Then I put my hand on her; she felt cold. I put my hand on her hand; it felt cold. And it looked like muddy looking stuff, had run from her eyes. And her--had her false teeth taken out, and her lips was drawed in and her mouth setting back like that, her face real thin. And she was… had her eyes set right back like that, and she was laying that way. And I took a hold of her. I shook her.
Seemingly she was dead, whether she was I do not know. God is my judge, I couldn't say. But when I took ahold of her right hand, there went that cancer. Well I knew then, I think now if the woman had been dead the cancer would have left too. Now, I--I believe it would. Now, that's the reason I think she was in--in a coma. So I was holding her hand, and it was hitting, I could hear, feel it hitting like that; turning, hitting. So then I said, "Dad, let's pray."
And he said, "All right, Brother Branham." And he got down on his knees, and began to calling, saying, "God, why did you let her go like that? Why couldn't she just have lived a little while longer?"
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Greit." Jeg tok hånden hennes. Nå, jeg vet ikke om kvinnen var død eller ikke. Gud er mitt vitne… Jeg tror hun var i koma. Jeg er ikke sikker. Selv om jeg ikke kunne kjenne hjerteslagene hennes, eller noe annet.
Men hør nå. Jeg la hånden min på henne; hun føltes kald. Jeg la hånden min på hennes hånd; den føltes kald. Det så ut som en mudderaktig væske hadde rent fra øynene hennes. Hun hadde fått fjernet gebisset, og leppene hennes var trukket inn, med munnen åpen bakover, og ansiktet veldig tynt. Øynene hennes var innsunkne og hun lå på denne måten. Jeg tok tak i henne og ristet henne.
Tilsynelatende var hun død, men jeg vet ikke sikkert. Gud er min dommer, jeg kan ikke si det. Men da jeg tok hennes høyre hånd, kjente jeg at kreften forsvant. Jeg visste da at selv om kvinnen hadde vært død, ville kreften ha forsvunnet også. Derfor tror jeg hun var i koma. Jeg holdt hennes hånd, og jeg kunne høre og føle det slå. Så sa jeg: "Pappa, la oss be."
Han sa: "Greit, Bror Branham." Han gikk ned på kne og begynte å be: "Gud, hvorfor lot Du henne gå slik? Hvorfor kunne hun ikke ha levd litt lenger?"

text:
Now, after hearing him praying, I started crying myself. I couldn't help it. Poor old fellow, I looked at him, his old hands was raising up to God, and he's feeble and trembling.
And I said, "Heavenly Father, You Who brought again Jesus from the dead," I said, "be merciful. Comfort the brother's heart." I kept on. I kept on praying. He was just screaming at the top of his voice. And I said, "Father, be merciful and heal her." And the vibration of the cancer stopped.
Well, Satan said to me then, "She died then."
Well, I just kept holding on. I said, "Lord, I believe that You brought up Lazarus from the grave. I believe You give the woman, the widow, her son again. I believe You laid Your hands upon Jairus' daughter and she came to life. Now, You're here. You can spare her life. And I'm just looking to You, Father. I pray that You'd hear me," like that.
text_no:
Etter å ha hørt ham be, begynte jeg selv å gråte. Jeg kunne ikke la være. Den stakkars gamle mannen, jeg så på ham, hans gamle hender løftet seg mot Gud, og han var svak og skjelvende.
Jeg sa: "Himmelske Far, Du som oppreiste Jesus fra de døde, vær nådig. Trøst brorens hjerte." Jeg fortsatte å be, mens han skrek for full hals. Jeg sa: "Far, vær nådig og helbred henne." Vibrationen fra kreften stoppet.
Satan sa til meg da: "Hun døde nå."
Jeg holdt bare fast. Jeg sa: "Herre, jeg tror at Du oppreiste Lazarus fra graven. Jeg tror Du gav enkens sønn tilbake til henne. Jeg tror Du la Dine hender på Jairus' datter, og hun kom til live. Nå er Du her. Du kan spare hennes liv. Og jeg ser opp til Deg, Far. Jeg ber at Du vil høre meg," slik.

text:
And I felt her grip my hand. I knowed she was alive then. I didn't say it. I just quit praying and watching him crying so much, he didn't hear me cease praying. And I looked, and I seen this skin on her forehead begin to wrinkling. I knowed life was in the woman. I just stood there a little bit on the side of the bed.
In a few moments, she was beginning to look around. Said, "Howdy do."
He done got down on the floor with his head on the floor, just a crying in the ambulance.
And I said, "Howdy do?"
She said, "Who are you?"
I said, "I'm Brother Branham."
She said, "Oh, you are?"
I said, "Set up."
And she set up, like that. He raised up, looked at her. He said, "Mother! Mother!" And he threw his arms around her and begin to hug her, like that. And they were just screaming both of them together, sitting in the ambulance. And I started to go out the door.
text_no:
Jeg kjente at hun grep hånden min. Jeg visste da at hun var i live. Jeg sa ikke noe, jeg bare sluttet å be. Han gråt så mye at han ikke merket at jeg hadde sluttet å be. Jeg så på henne og så at huden i pannen begynte å rynke. Jeg visste at det var liv i henne. Jeg sto bare stille ved siden av sengen en stund.
Etter noen øyeblikk begynte hun å se seg rundt og sa: "Hallo."
Han lå på gulvet med hodet ned og gråt i ambulansen.
Jeg sa: "Hallo."
Hun spurte: "Hvem er du?"
Jeg svarte: "Jeg er Bror Branham."
Hun sa: "Å, er du det?"
Jeg sa: "Sitt opp."
Og hun satte seg opp. Han løftet hodet, så på henne og ropte: "Mor! Mor!" Han kastet armene rundt henne og begynte å klemme henne. De skrek begge to, sittende i ambulansen. Deretter begynte jeg å gå ut av døren.

text:
And the driver set there, the intern, the driver. He said, "Brother Branham, you couldn't get out that door." Said, "They're just packed against there." Said, "I'll tell you what I'll do." Said, "I sent those men all around…"
Way back in the parking lot, standing full of people. And some of them hadn't left there for eight days. It was drizzling rain. And so they had this…
Set right in the seat. Anyone on the inside, stayed there. They let somebody go out and get them a hamburger or something, come in. They'd eat right there. They wouldn't move. They stayed right there, slept right in their seats.
And he--he just started to unbutton his coat, like this, and hold it back at the door, like he was taking his coat off. Said, "Now when I do that you go out the other side. And they're going to meet you down there at the end."
I said, "All right." That looked like a hypocritical deed, but I just couldn't… I had to do it. I couldn't get back to the auditorium.
text_no:
Sjåføren, internsjåføren, sa: "Bror Branham, du kan ikke gå ut den døren." Han sa: "Det er helt fullt der ute." Han sa: "Jeg skal fortelle deg hva jeg gjør. Jeg har sendt de mennene rundt..."
Bakerst på parkeringsplassen sto det fullt av folk. Noen av dem hadde ikke forlatt stedet på åtte dager. Det småregnet. Så de var der...
De satt rett i setene. Alle på innsiden ble der. De lot noen gå ut og hente en hamburger eller noe annet til dem. De spiste rett der. De rørte seg ikke, sov i setene sine.
Han begynte å kneppe opp frakken sin, som dette, og holdt den foran døren, som om han tok den av. Han sa: "Når jeg gjør dette, går du ut på den andre siden. De møter deg der nede ved enden."
Jeg sa: "Greit." Det virket hyklersk, men jeg måtte gjøre det. Jeg kunne ikke komme tilbake til auditoriet.

text:
And I went out the side, like that, the other side which was near the fence. I went down along that row of ambulances there. And way down, come around the end of the alley, come into a big parking lot. And just about as far as three, four times this tent. And it just milling full of people.
Well, I thought, "Where are those people at?" And no one knew me. They hadn't seen me back there. So I started pushing through, trying to get up towards the door. I thought maybe they'd be up there towards the door. The big lights out there. And I--I was going, pushing through, like this. And everybody hollering, "Set down. Quit pushing."
I just kept on going, you know. And I… "Quit pushing." And I just kept on going.
After while, I… There was a big fellow standing there, whittling with his knife. I kinda stepped up against him, like that. I said, "Pardon me."
He said, "I said, 'Quit pushing.'"
I was afraid he was going to push. So I--I did stop. And so I said, "Yes, sir. Excuse me, sir." I stood there a little bit.
He was looking down at me, kept on whittling, talking to the other fellow.
text_no:
Jeg gikk ut på siden, nær gjerdet. Jeg fulgte raden med ambulanser nedover. Etter en stund kom jeg rundt enden av smugveien og inn på en stor parkeringsplass. Det var like langt som tre-fire ganger denne teltplassen. Det var fullt av folk.
Jeg tenkte: "Hvor er de folkene hen?" Ingen kjente meg, for de hadde ikke sett meg der bak. Så jeg begynte å presse meg gjennom mengden for å komme nærmere døren. Jeg trodde kanskje de var der ved døren under de store lysene. Jeg presset meg fremover, mens folk ropte: "Sett deg ned. Slutt å dytte."
Jeg fortsatte bare å gå. "Slutt å dytte," ropte de, men jeg ga meg ikke.
Etter en stund sto en stor kar der, han spikket med en kniv. Jeg kom nær ham og sa: "Unnskyld meg."
Han svarte: "Jeg sa, 'Slutt å dytte.'"
Jeg ble redd for å bli dyttet, så jeg stanset. "Ja, herr. Unnskyld meg," sa jeg og ble stående en stund.
Han så ned på meg mens han fortsatte å spikke og snakke med den andre karen.

text:
I looked at him a little bit till he got his head turned good. And I looked around to see where the door was. It's quite a distance yet to the door. I couldn't see no one coming for me yet.
So I heard a cry. I heard somebody hollering, "Daddy! Daddy! Oh, daddy." The most pathetic cry.
I looked around to see where it was. Coming down through the audience come a well dressed colored girl, just as blind as she could be. Her eyes were just as white with cataracts nearly as my shirt. She was coming along like this, nobody noticing her, coming along, like that, hollering, "Daddy! Oh, daddy!"
And I watched her. And I thought, "Well now, isn't that amazing. Look like somebody would help that poor girl. Regardless of what they think about it; they--they ought to help that girl some way." I thought, "I believe I'll help her."
text_no:
Jeg så på ham litt til før han vendte hodet tilstrekkelig. Jeg kikket rundt for å se hvor døren var. Den var fortsatt et stykke unna. Jeg kunne ikke se noen komme for å hente meg ennå.
Så hørte jeg et rop. Jeg hørte noen rope: "Pappa! Pappa! Å, pappa." Det mest hjerteskjærende ropet.
Jeg snudde meg for å se hvor det kom fra. Gjennom publikum kom en pent kledd farget jente, like blind som det går an. Øynene hennes var like hvite av grå stær som skjorten min. Hun beveget seg fremover, uten at noen la merke til henne, og ropte: "Pappa! Å, pappa!"
Jeg fulgte med på henne og tenkte: "Det er utrolig. Det er jo noen som burde hjelpe den stakkars jenta. Uansett hva de mener om det, så bør de hjelpe henne." Jeg bestemte meg for at jeg ville hjelpe henne.

text:
I didn't see no one. So I started moving out to her. And I thought, "You know, maybe the Lord will heal her." So I begin to think about that. And I heard her crying. And I… She was crying. And I walked out. And I thought, "I'll just try her faith, just get a conversation with her." And I… Ever which way she was going, I'd make myself get right in her way.
And finally, she bumped into me. And she said, "Excuse me, suh."
And I said… That's southern talk, you know, "Suh." And I said, "Yes, ma'am.
And she backed off. She said, "Daddy!"
I thought, "Now, I'll just see what kind of faith you have…?…" That looked like a hypocrite again, to do that. And I said, "Ah, what do you want?"
She said, "Suh, I lost my daddy. I cain't find him no whuh."
I said, "Look like you're blind."
Said, "Yes, suh. I's blind."
Nice talking lady. And I said, "Well, ah, I don't see no more colored people around here." I said, "Where you from?"
She said, "I's from Memphis."
text_no:
Jeg så ingen. Så jeg begynte å bevege meg mot henne og tenkte: "Kanskje Herren vil helbrede henne." Jeg begynte å tenke på det og hørte henne gråte. Jeg gikk mot henne og tenkte: "Jeg skal prøve hennes tro ved å få henne til å snakke med meg." Uansett hvilken vei hun gikk, sørget jeg for å komme i veien for henne.
Til slutt støtte hun på meg og sa: "Unnskyld, sir."
Jeg svarte: "Ja, frue."
Hun trakk seg tilbake og ropte: "Pappa!"
Jeg tenkte: "Nå skal jeg se hva slags tro du har." Det føltes litt hyklersk, men jeg sa: "Hva vil du?"
Hun svarte: "Sir, jeg har mistet pappaen min. Jeg kan ikke finne ham noe sted."
Jeg sa: "Det ser ut som du er blind."
Hun svarte: "Ja, sir. Jeg er blind."
Hun var en høflig dame, og jeg sa: "Vel, jeg ser ingen andre fargede mennesker her rundt. Hvor kommer du fra?"
Hun svarte: "Jeg er fra Memphis."

text:
And I looked over, and I seen all that bunch of chartered busses setting there. I seen one of them had Memphis on it. I thought, "Well, I could get her over to that bus anyhow." So I said… if the people didn't begin to recognize me coming in. So I said, "Well, what are you doing over here?" Just to test her faith…
She said, "I come over to see the healah."
I said, "The what?"
She said, "The healah."
And I said, "You don't believe that, do you?"
She said, "Oh, yes, suh."
Oh, my. I begin to feel something stir in my heart.
And she said, "I listen to all the good radio programs." She said, "This morning, I heard people testifying from this place that had never talked before in their life." And she said, "I heard of a blind man from up in Missour' that received his sight." Said, "Sir, I come over to receive my sight."
My, I thought, "Oh, I'm a hypocrite, Lord." I said, "Why am I bump into her like that?" I thought, "Well, I'll go on through with it." I said, "Do you believe in that?"
She said, "Yes, suh!" She said, "I believes it with all my heart."
text_no:
Jeg så over og la merke til alle de chartrede bussene som sto der. Jeg så at en av dem hadde "Memphis" på seg. Jeg tenkte, "Vel, jeg kunne i det minste få henne over til den bussen." Så jeg sa... om ikke folk begynte å kjenne meg igjen når jeg kom inn. Så jeg sa, "Hva gjør du her borte?" Bare for å teste troen hennes...
Hun svarte, "Jeg kom for å se helbrederen."
Jeg sa, "Hva sa du?"
Hun svarte, "Helbrederen."
Og jeg sa, "Du tror vel ikke på det, gjør du?"
Hun svarte, "Å, jo, sir."
Å, min. Jeg begynte å kjenne noe røre seg i hjertet mitt.
Hun fortsatte, "Jeg lytter til alle de gode radioprogrammene." Hun sa, "I morges hørte jeg folk som vitnet fra dette stedet som aldri hadde snakket før i sitt liv." Og hun sa, "Jeg hørte om en blind mann fra Missouri som fikk synet tilbake." Hun sa, "Sir, jeg kom for å få synet mitt tilbake."
Jeg tenkte, "Å, jeg er en hykler, Herre." Jeg sa, "Hvorfor oppfører jeg meg slik mot henne?" Jeg tenkte, "Vel, jeg får fortsette." Jeg sa, "Tror du på det?"
Hun svarte, "Ja, sir!" Hun sa, "Jeg tror på det av hele mitt hjerte."

text:
I said, "In the day… How'd you come to go blind?"
She said, "When I was a little girl, the doctor told me I had cataracts." And said, "He said, 'When they got ripe…'" Now, I don't know what "ripe" means yet. But said, "When they got ripe, he would take them out." And said, "Now, that they got ripe, he said they done went down and wrapped around the optical nerve of my eyes. If he'd pull the cataract, he'd pull my eyes out." Said, "My only hope is to get to the man. And they tell me this is his last night here. And I can't even get near the building. And I've lost my daddy, and I don't know what to do. Nobody will help me." She said, "Could you help me to the bus, sir?"
I said, "Yes, ma'am." But I said, "First, about this healing…" I said, "Did you hear that man testify about that Angel coming down from heaven?"
She said, "Yes, suh."
I said, "Do you believe that?"
She said, "With all my heart."
I said, "In the day when we've got all the best medical science and the best doctors we ever had, and you mean to say that God Almighty would do something like that?"
She said, "Yes, suh. I believes it."
text_no:
Jeg spurte, "Hvordan ble du blind?"
Hun svarte, "Da jeg var liten jente, sa legen at jeg hadde grå stær. Han sa, 'Når de modner…'" Jeg vet fortsatt ikke hva "modner" betyr i denne sammenhengen. Hun fortsatte, "Når de modner, skulle han fjerne dem. Nå som de har modnet, sier han at de har gått ned og viklet seg rundt synsnerven i øynene mine. Hvis han fjerner den grå stæren, vil han rive ut øynene mine." Hun sa, "Min eneste håp er å komme til denne mannen. De sier at dette er hans siste kveld her, og jeg kan ikke engang komme nær bygningen. Jeg har mistet faren min, og jeg vet ikke hva jeg skal gjøre. Ingen vil hjelpe meg." Hun spurte, "Kan du hjelpe meg til bussen, sir?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja, madam." Men jeg la til, "Først, om denne helbredelsen… Hørte du den mannen vitne om at en engel kom ned fra himmelen?"
Hun sa, "Ja, sir."
Jeg spurte, "Tror du på det?"
Hun svarte, "Med hele mitt hjerte."
Jeg sa, "I denne tiden, når vi har den beste medisinske vitenskapen og de beste legene noensinne, mener du at Gud Den Allmektige ville gjøre noe sånt?"
Hun svarte, "Ja, sir. Jeg tror det."

text:
And I… Now, she couldn't see me. She was totally blind--had been since she was twelve years old, and she was about twenty then, I guess.
And said… And I said, "Are you totally blind?"
She said, "Yes, suh. I couldn't tell daylight from dark." Said, "I haven't for years."
And I said, "Well, look. Do you… Why don't…" I said, "We--we got good doctors."
She said, "But, sir, they can't help me." She said, "I'll tell you what I'll do." She said, "If you'll get me into the building where that man's at, I'll find my daddy after that." Oh, my. That was too much for me. I couldn't stand that. She said, "You get me in to where he's at, and I'll find my daddy." She said, "There's a blind man been blind twelve years, this morning received his sight. And sir, won't you help me get where he's at, that I might receive my sight?"
And I thought of poor old blind Fanny Crosby:
Pass me not, O gentle Saviour,
Hear my humble cry;
While on others Thou art calling,
Do not pass me by.
text_no:
Nå, hun kunne ikke se meg. Hun var helt blind—hadde vært det siden hun var tolv år gammel, og nå var hun omkring tjue, antar jeg.
Jeg spurte, "Er du helt blind?"
Hun svarte, "Ja, herr. Jeg kan ikke skille mellom dag og natt." Hun fortsatte, "Det har jeg ikke kunnet på mange år."
Jeg sa, "Vel, se her. Hvorfor... Vi har gode leger."
Hun sa, "Men, herr, de kan ikke hjelpe meg." Hun fortsatte, "Jeg skal fortelle deg hva jeg vil gjøre. Hvis du får meg inn i bygningen der den mannen er, skal jeg finne faren min etterpå." Å, det ble for mye for meg. Jeg kunne ikke stå imot det. Hun sa, "Hjelp meg å komme inn dit han er, så skal jeg finne faren min." Hun tilføyde, "En blind mann som hadde vært blind i tolv år, fikk synet sitt tilbake i dag morges. Vil du ikke hjelpe meg å komme dit han er, så jeg kan få synet mitt?"
Jeg tenkte på stakkars, gamle, blinde Fanny Crosby:
Pass me not, O gentle Saviour,
Hear my humble cry;
While on others Thou art calling,
Do not pass me by.

text:
And I looked; my heart was just a breaking. I said, "Look, lady. Perhaps I'm the one you want to see."
And friends, I'll never forget. Those hands grabbed me by the lapel, like that. She said, "Is you the healah?"
I said, "No. I'm Brother Branham." I said, "Jesus is the Healer."
And she held me, like that. She said, "Oh, don't pass me. Have mercy."
And I said, "Sister, 'course, I would do all I could as a man. And I believe Jesus will hear our prayer." I said, "Do you believe Him?"
She said, "With all my heart, sir."
I said, "Now, let me have your hand."
Do you think she'd turn loose of my coat? No, sir. She's holding right there. She said, "I isn't going to turn you loose, suh." She said, "Is…"
And I said, "Well look, sister. Let me have your right hand. And you can hold with your left. But I want to feel the vibration of those cataracts to know whether they're dead or not."
text_no:
Jeg så det, og hjertet mitt var knust. Jeg sa: "Se her, frue. Kanskje det er meg du vil snakke med."
Og venner, jeg vil aldri glemme det. De hendene grep meg i jakkeslaget. Hun sa: "Er du helbrederen?"
Jeg svarte: "Nei. Jeg er Bror Branham. Jesus er Helbrederen."
Hun holdt fast i meg. Hun sa: "Å, ikke gå forbi meg. Ha nåde."
Jeg sa: "Søster, selvfølgelig vil jeg gjøre alt jeg kan som menneske. Og jeg tror Jesus vil høre vår bønn." Jeg spurte: "Tror du på Ham?"
Hun svarte: "Av hele mitt hjerte, sir."
Jeg sa: "La meg få holde hånden din."
Tror du hun slapp taket i jakken min? Nei, sir. Hun holdt fast. Hun sa: "Jeg kommer ikke til å slippe deg, sir." Hun sa: "Er …"
Jeg sa: "Se her, søster. La meg få høyre hånden din. Du kan holde fast med venstre. Men jeg må kjenne vibrasjonen av de grå stærne for å vite om de er borte eller ikke."

text:
I finally got her to let me have her hand. I took ahold of it, and there they was. I said, "Now, so that nobody will notice us," I said, "you bow your head now. Don't raise your head till I tell you to. And believe with all your heart."
She said, "I do, suh." She bowed her head.
Here's the way I was praying. I'll never forget the way I prayed. I said, "Heavenly Father, some nineteen hundred years ago, there was an old rugged cross dragging down through the streets of Jerusalem. It was dragging out the bloody footprints of the Bearer. On the road up to Golgotha, His little, weak, frail body fell under the load. Along came Simon, the Cyrene, colored man, Ethiopian, picked up the cross and helped Him bear the cross." Said, "Father, I'm sure You understand. And here's one of his daughters tonight, staggering in darkness. Won't You help her?"
text_no:
Til slutt fikk jeg henne til å la meg holde hånden hennes. Jeg tok tak i den, og der var de. Jeg sa: "Nå, for at ingen skal legge merke til oss," sa jeg, "bøy hodet ditt nå. Ikke løft det før jeg sier ifra. Og tro av hele ditt hjerte."
Hun svarte, "Det gjør jeg, sir." Hun bøyde hodet.
Slik ba jeg. Jeg vil aldri glemme hvordan jeg ba. Jeg sa: "Himmelske Far, for omkring nitten hundre år siden, var det et gammelt, røft kors som ble dratt ned gjennom Jerusalems gater. Det fulgte de blodige fotsporene til Bæreren. På vei opp til Golgata falt Hans lille, svake, skjøre kropp under byrden. Da kom Simon fra Kyrene, en farget mann, en etiopier, og hjalp Ham bære korset." Jeg sa, "Far, jeg er sikker på at Du forstår. Og her er en av hans døtre i kveld, snublende i mørket. Vil Du ikke hjelpe henne?"

text:
No more than I said that, the vibrations ceased on my hand. I said, "Now, keep your head bowed, sister, just a moment." And she kept her head bowed. I waited a minute for the shrinkage on the cataract. I said, "Now, keep your eyelids closed until you get your face about even with mine… your judgment."
Said, "Yes, suh." Said, "I feel real cool."
I said, "Just don't open your eyes." When she got about even, I said, "Now, receive your sight, for Jesus has healed you."
She opened her eyes. She said, "It took… Is them lights?"
I said, "Yes, ma'am."
Said, "Is that people passing?" She said, "O Lord, I who once was blind, can now see." And she screamed out. She fell forward.
And the people begin to notice. And here they come, running together. And I started to run.
I heard… I looked coming down, just a little bit before I started praying; I seen a man pressing through the crowd, hunting for me. And I started towards them.
text_no:
Så snart jeg sa det, opphørte vibrasjonene i hånden min. Jeg sa, "Hold hodet bøyd, søster, et øyeblikk." Hun holdt hodet bøyd. Jeg ventet et minutt på at gråstær skulle trekke seg sammen. Jeg sa, "Hold øynene lukket til ansiktet ditt er omtrent på høyde med mitt... din vurdering."
Hun sa, "Ja, herre." Og la til, "Jeg føler meg veldig kjølig."
Jeg sa, "Bare ikke åpne øynene." Da hun kom litt nærmere, sa jeg, "Nå, motta ditt syn, for Jesus har helbredet deg."
Hun åpnet øynene og sa, "Det fungerte... Er det lys?"
Jeg svarte, "Ja, frue."
Hun sa, "Er det folk som går forbi?" Og la til, "Å Herre, jeg som en gang var blind, kan nå se." Hun skrek ut og falt fremover.
Folk begynte å legge merke til det, og de kom løpende sammen. Jeg begynte å løpe.
Jeg hørte... Jeg så noen komme før jeg startet å be; jeg så en mann presse seg gjennom mengden, letende etter meg. Jeg beveget meg mot dem.

text:
Just as I started towards them, there was an old brother. I never will forget. He--he had a--a club. He had a crooked foot. He was standing like this. He said, "I know you, Brother Branham." He said, "I've been standing in this rain for eight days." Said, "Tell me what to do, and God will back your word."
I said, "You, with all your heart, you believe?"
He said, "With all my heart."
I said, "Throw away your club and be whole in the Name of the Lord Jesus."
He threw his club away and made out a scream. And up in the air he went, jumped. Down he come, just as normal as any man, begin to screaming.
text_no:
Akkurat i det jeg gikk mot dem, var det en eldre bror som sto der. Jeg vil aldri glemme det. Han hadde en stokk og en skeiv fot. Han sto slik. Han sa, "Jeg kjenner deg, Bror Branham." Han sa, "Jeg har stått i dette regnet i åtte dager. Fortell meg hva jeg skal gjøre, så vil Gud stadfeste Ditt ord."
Jeg spurte, "Tror du, med hele ditt hjerte?"
Han svarte, "Med hele mitt hjerte."
Jeg sa, "Kast bort stokken din og bli helbredet i Herrens Jesu Navn."
Han kastet bort stokken, utstøtte et skrik og hoppet opp i luften. Da han landet igjen, var han like normal som hvilken som helst annen mann, og begynte å rope av glede.

text:
And friends, I started to push towards these men. There's no harm in saying this. When I first started, my brother gave me a suit to go out in, he'd had a wreck it, and tore it in several places. And my wife and I went down to the ten cent store and got some of them patches you iron on with an iron, you know. And I don't know what they are. And I sewed the pocket up, where it had tore down here on the side, down along the side. Took a needle… And I'm not very much of a seamstress. So I sewed it up.
And when I would go and meet ministers or anything, I was ashamed of that ragged coat. So I'd hold it sideways and put my right hand out, like this, and shake hands with my left hand, and say, "Well, I… Excuse my left hand, but it's closer to my heart." See? Just like that, to say that, instead of letting them see that old ragged coat.
text_no:
Venner, jeg begynte å nærme meg disse mennene. Det gjør ikke noe å si dette. Da jeg først startet, ga broren min meg en dress å bruke. Han hadde hatt en ulykke med den og revet den flere steder. Min kone og jeg gikk ned til ti-kroners-butikken og kjøpte noen slike lapper man kan stryke på med et strykejern. Jeg vet egentlig ikke hva de er, men jeg sydde lommen der den hadde revnet på siden. Jeg tok en nål... Og jeg er ikke særlig god til å sy. Så jeg sydde den opp.
Når jeg møtte forkynnere eller andre, skammet jeg meg over den fillete jakken. Jeg ville holde den til siden, strekke ut høyre hånd og håndhilse med venstre hånd mens jeg sa: "Unnskyld min venstre hånd, men den er nærmere hjertet mitt." På den måten unngikk jeg at de så den gamle, fillete jakken.

text:
But, brother, while I was trying to get to that men, God bless their gallant hearts, they were pushing through there, trying to get their little babies, trying to touch that old ragged coat, when I passing by. They're just throwing down their crutches, and people screaming and hollering, just so that they're try to touch the old coat.
Brother, it wasn't the old coat that done the healing; it was their faith in Jesus Christ that done the healing.
And that same Christ, that was in Jonesboro that night, is here in Cleveland tonight to perform any miracle, to do anything that faith will rise to require it to be done. Do you believe that?
text_no:
Men, bror, mens jeg forsøkte å nå fram til de mennene, Gud velsigne deres tapre hjerter, trengte de seg gjennom mengden og prøvde å få sine små babyer til å berøre den gamle, fillete frakken da jeg passerte forbi. De kastet krykkene sine og folk skrek og ropte, alt for å prøve å berøre den gamle frakken.
Bror, det var ikke den gamle frakken som utførte helbredelsen; det var deres tro på Jesus Kristus som gjorde det.
Og den samme Kristus, som var i Jonesboro den kvelden, er her i Cleveland i kveld for å utføre ethvert mirakel, for å gjøre alt som troen krever. Tror du det?

text:
Let us bow our heads. Heavenly Father, I'm thinking of that glorious, marvelous night, that poor colored girl; thinking of a few weeks later from then, stopping there in the airplane hanger, someone walking up and saying, "Remember me, Brother Branham?" O God, someday when it's all over and we've gathered to the other side, what a marvelous time it'll be to set down with those redeemed.
Here we are tonight, Lord, way across the country here in Cleveland, Ohio, been across the blue waters to the other lands down to the needy. We're still struggling on, not weary in the way. O God, believing You for all things. Seeing You here to heal the sick and the afflicted, bringing this morning glorious powers, manifesting Yourself in great healings, interpreting the hearts of the people, telling their diseases, the secret sins and thoughts of their mind, healing the paralyzed, the lame, the deaf, the dumb.
O Christ of God, this great night, it'll be history after while, if Thou tarry. Help us now to move in with real faith. Grant it, Lord. May the people repent of all that's done that's wrong. Accept us now in the Beloved, for we ask it in His Name. Amen.
text_no:
La oss bøye våre hoder. Himmelske Far, jeg tenker på den herlige, vidunderlige kvelden, på den fattige fargede jenta; tenker på noen uker senere, når vi stoppet der i flyhangaren. Noen kom bort og sa: "Husker du meg, Bror Branham?" O Gud, en dag når alt er over, og vi har samlet oss på den andre siden, hvilken vidunderlig tid det vil være å sitte ned med de forløste.
Her er vi i kveld, Herre, langt borte her i Cleveland, Ohio, etter å ha reist over de blå hav til andre land for å møte de trengende. Vi fortsetter å kjempe, ikke trette på veien. O Gud, vi tror på Deg for alt. Vi ser Deg her for å helbrede de syke og plaget, og bringe herlige krefter i dag morges, manifestere Deg i store helbredelser, tolke folks hjerter, og fortelle om deres sykdommer, deres hemmelige synder og tanker, helbrede de lamme, døve og stumme.
O Kristus av Gud, denne store kvelden, den vil bli historie etter en stund, om Du drøyer. Hjelp oss nå til å komme i sann tro. Gi det, Herre. Må folket angre på alt galt de har gjort. Aksepter oss nå i Den Elskede, for vi ber i Hans Navn. Amen.

text:
[A brother calls the prayer line]
Oh, isn't He wonderful, glorious? While the prayer line's being formed, believe now with all of your heart. We'll wait first till we get some out.
You don't have to have a prayer card. That doesn't have a thing to do with it. That prayer card is merely to get people lined up. They give out a hundred each day. And when they do, I think it's twe--fifty at morning and fifty in the afternoon. They bring the prayer cards up, and shuffle them out, hand them to each person and they write down their diseases. And you bring up… That's merely to keep you in line. That doesn't have one thing to do… God bless this couple coming here.
All right. He's here to perform any miracle, do anything that you believe that He will do. Just have faith in Him; believe Him.
text_no:
[En bror kaller på bønnekøen]
Å, er ikke Han vidunderlig og herlig? Mens bønnekøen formes, tro nå av hele ditt hjerte. Vi venter litt til flere kommer fram.
Du trenger ikke et bønnekort. Det har ingenting med saken å gjøre. Bønnekortet er kun for å organisere køen. De deler ut hundre hver dag, femti om morgenen og femti om ettermiddagen. De samler inn bønnekortene, blander dem og deler dem ut til hver enkelt person som skriver ned sine sykdommer på kortene. Det er kun for å holde orden i køen. Det har ingenting å si... Gud velsigne dette paret som kommer her.
Greit. Han er her for å utføre ethvert mirakel, gjøre alt du tror Han vil gjøre. Ha tro på Ham; tro på Ham.

text:
Now, right from the platform here, many, many times, many times God heals the sick and the needy. God can reveal right here from this platform what you have need of, if you'll just believe. Do you do it with all your heart? Have faith. You have faith and God has mercy. See? And by His mercy, He'll heal you, make you well. Believe with all your heart just now.
Now, here it is. What… You know what I'm trying to do right now, don't you? How many realizes that? See? I'm waiting for the anointing to strike me. That's right. When I feeled It…
I know it's here at the platform… Friends, God knows what's true. Sometimes I don't see it. But look, while I explain it. It's just a pressure, like, in the--like in mid-air. If It went this way, you could tell it. If It come this way, you could tell it. It's right… You could tell. It's just like a form of a man. And now, It's to my extreme back, right here.
